module.title: Genre: House
module.description: Build authentic house music from the ground up. Master four-on-the-floor drum programming, chord stabs, funky basslines, and the groove techniques that make house feel irresistible on the dancefloor.

lessons.title: The House Foundation: Four-on-the-Floor
lessons.body:
House music is built on a single, undeniable rhythmic idea: the four-on-the-floor kick drum. A kick on every beat, without exception, at 120–130 BPM, creates a pulse that is simultaneously simple and hypnotic. But the four-on-the-floor kick alone doesn't make house music — it's how everything else relates to that pulse that defines the genre.

## What Four-on-the-Floor Means

In a 4/4 bar at 125 BPM, four-on-the-floor means a kick drum hits on beats 1, 2, 3, and 4 — every quarter note, without rest. This creates a perfectly steady forward motion that propels the dancefloor. It is the rhythmic backbone of house, techno, disco, and many EDM subgenres.

The power of the four-on-the-floor is not the pattern itself (which is extremely simple) — it's the **consistency**. After 8 bars of identical four-on-the-floor, the listener's body is locked in. The variations around the kick (the offbeat clap, the syncopated hi-hats, the rising bassline) create all the musical interest, but the kick never wavers.

## The Classic House Drum Pattern

**Kick**: Beats 1, 2, 3, 4 (every quarter note)
**Open hi-hat or clap**: Beats 2 and 4 (the backbeat)
**Closed hi-hat**: The offbeats — the "and" of every beat (the 8th-note offbeats: 1+, 2+, 3+, 4+)
**Additional percussion**: Congas, cowbells, shakers — added for texture

In 16-step notation (each step = 1/16 note):
```
Kick:   [1] . . . [5] . . . [9] . . . [13] . . .
Clap:   . . . . [5] . . . . . . . [13] . . . .
Hi-hat: . . [3] . . . [7] . . . [11] . . . [15] .
```

The closed hi-hat on the 8th-note offbeats is what creates the rhythmic lift between kick hits — it's what makes house feel bouncy rather than heavy.

## Sample Choice for the House Kick

The house kick is specific. It needs:

- **Punchy transient**: A sharp click at the very start, followed by a body thump
- **Short to medium decay**: The kick should ring for about 100–200 ms, not a long booming tail
- **Low end weight**: A presence at 60–100 Hz but not extreme sub (unlike trap's 808)
- **Tuned pitch**: Many house producers tune their kick to the key of the track — a kick that's in the right pitch resonates sympathetically with the bassline

A classic house kick comes from the Roland TR-909 drum machine. The 909 kick has a characteristic click transient and a rounded body that has defined house music since the mid-1980s. 909 samples are freely available and are a legitimate starting point.

## The Open Hi-Hat: House's Defining Texture

The open hi-hat (or "ride cymbal" in some productions) on beats 2 and 4 is as important as the kick in defining house. But the real texture comes from **closed hi-hat patterns** around the offbeats.

A classic variation: place two 16th notes on each offbeat (both the 8th-note offbeat and the 16th note just before it). This creates a double-hit that feels like it's pushing forward into each beat — a propulsive swing motion that makes house feel like it's constantly moving toward the next kick hit.

```
Kick:        [1] . . . [5] . . . [9] . . . [13] . . .
Open hat:    . . . . [5] . . . . . . . [13] . . . .
Closed hat:  . [2] [3] . . [6] [7] . . [10][11] . . [14][15].
```

The double closed hi-hat before beats 2 and 4 creates a stutter that accelerates into the clap — a feeling of rhythmic anticipation that is quintessentially house.

## Kick and Open Hi-Hat Choke

In real music, when a kick drum hits, the energy of the drum often cuts or shortens the hi-hat. Simulate this by shortening the open hi-hat note length or by programming a closed hi-hat on the same step as any kick that occurs where a hat was playing. This prevents the hi-hat from bleeding over the kick punch.

## Programming Variations for Energy Management

Four-on-the-floor over 8+ minutes needs variation to hold the listener. Within the same kick + hat foundation, vary:

- **Remove one kick hit** per 4 bars (on beat 4 of the last bar before a section) — creates a micro-drop that emphasizes the return
- **Add a double kick** (kick on beat 4 and 4.5) at phrase endings — the "pump" feeling
- **Change hi-hat density**: Sparse (just offbeats) in the intro, dense (every 16th note) at the peak
- **Add an open hat accent** on a different 16th note position each 4 bars

## Setting Up in FL Studio

In the Step Sequencer:
1. Load your kick sample on channel 1. Enable steps 1, 5, 9, 13 (beats 1, 2, 3, 4).
2. Load your clap/snare on channel 2. Enable steps 5 and 13.
3. Load a closed hi-hat on channel 3. Enable steps 3, 7, 11, 15 (8th-note offbeats).
4. Load an open hi-hat on channel 4. Enable steps 5 and 13. Set note length to short (1 beat).

Set the BPM to **125**. Press Play. That's the foundation of every house track ever made.
keyTakeaways: Four-on-the-floor means a kick on every quarter note — its power comes from absolute consistency, not complexity. Never remove kick hits mid-pattern except for deliberate drops.
The closed hi-hat on the 8th-note offbeats creates the bouncy lift between kicks — the double hi-hat just before beats 2 and 4 is the classic house rhythmic push.
Vary energy by changing hi-hat density across sections rather than changing the kick pattern — the kick stays constant while everything around it evolves.
lessons.tryThis: Set BPM to 125. In the Step Sequencer, programme the classic house pattern: kick on beats 1-2-3-4, clap on 2 and 4, closed hi-hat on the 8th offbeats. Listen for 8 bars, then add the double hi-hat before beats 2 and 4 (steps 4+5 and 12+13 in a 16-step sequencer). Listen to how the feel changes. Finally, on bar 4, remove the kick on beat 4 step — notice the brief void and how the kick landing back on beat 1 of bar 5 hits harder. That's the micro-drop technique.

lessons.title: House Chord Stabs and Pads
lessons.body:
If the kick drum is the spine of house music, chords are its soul. From the gospel-influenced organ stabs of Chicago house to the pumping synthesiser chords of modern progressive house, harmonic elements define the emotional character of a track. House chord programming combines rhythm, harmony, and production into a single technique that is both approachable and deep.

## The Chord Stab

A **stab** is a short, rhythmically precise chord hit — typically 1/16 or 1/8 note length. Stabs are rhythmically active, punching between the kick and hi-hats to create a syncopated groove. The rhythm of the stabs is as important as the chord itself.

Classic house stab rhythms:

**Offbeat stabs**: Land on the 8th-note offbeats (the "and" of each beat). These lock with the hi-hat offbeats and push against the four-on-the-floor kick.

**Syncopated stabs**: Land on the 4th 16th note of a beat (the "e" of beat 1 = step 4 in a 16-step bar) — slightly before the next beat, creating a pushing forward motion.

**2-against-3 polyrhythm**: A stab pattern where 3 stabs occur every 2 beats creates a looping cross-rhythm that feels constantly moving.

## Chord Types in House

House draws heavily from gospel, soul, and disco — which means the chord vocabulary is rich:

| Chord | Character | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Major 7 | Bright, resolved | Uplifting house progressions |
| Minor 7 | Mellow, soulful | Deeper, emotional house |
| Dominant 7 | Tension, bluesy | Funky house, transitions |
| Suspended 4 (sus4) | Open, unresolved | Creates expectation before resolution |
| Add9 | Warm, rich | Modern house, less traditional |
| Minor 9 | Deep soul | Deep house |

A house track might use only one chord (looped throughout with rhythmic variation) or a 4-chord progression. One-chord house creates hypnotic intensity; multi-chord house creates more harmonic storytelling.

## Programming Stabs in the Piano Roll

**Short notes**: Enter chord notes at 1/16 note length. All notes of the chord start on the same beat. The short duration creates the percussive stab quality — the chord attacks and then falls away before the next hit.

**Velocity for accent**: Stabs on the offbeat should be slightly softer (75–90) than stabs that land on the beat (100–115). This mirrors how a real keyboardist accents certain positions more than others.

**Staccato articulation**: For an especially short, pointed stab, shorten notes to 1/32 length. This creates a very percussive quality, almost like a high note on a percussion instrument.

**Example stab pattern (4 bars, Cmaj7, 125 BPM)**:

```
Bar 1: stab on beat 1+, beat 2+, beat 4
Bar 2: stab on beat 1, beat 2+, beat 3+
Bar 3: same as bar 1
Bar 4: stab on beat 1, then a rapid 4-note fill on beats 3-4 (16th note stabs)
```

The variation in bar 4 acts as a rhythmic fill leading back into bar 1.

## House Pads vs Stabs

Where stabs are rhythmically active and short, **pads** are sustained, evolving textures that provide harmonic warmth in the background. Most house tracks use both:

- **Stabs**: Rhythmic foreground, punchy, syncopated
- **Pads**: Harmonic background, sustained, often with slow filter sweep automation

Pad characteristics for house:

- **Slow attack**: 300–600 ms attack time. The pad swells in rather than attacking sharply. This prevents pads from clashing rhythmically with the stabs.
- **Sustained hold**: Pads hold for the entire chord duration (typically 1 or 2 bars per chord)
- **Detuning**: Slightly detuned unison voices (2–3 oscillators detuned by ±5–10 cents) create the lush, wide house pad sound
- **Cutoff sweep**: A slow LFO or automation clip on the filter cutoff makes the pad open and close over time — a classic house technique that adds movement without changing the notes

## The Pumping Effect (Sidechain)

One of the most distinctive house production techniques is the **pumping** effect: the pad volume ducks in rhythm with the kick, creating a pulsing, breathing quality that is synchronized to the four-on-the-floor. This is achieved with sidechain compression.

In FL Studio:
1. Add a compressor (Fruity Peak Controller or a compressor plugin) on the pad Mixer channel
2. Route the kick output to the sidechain input of this compressor
3. Set attack very fast (1–3 ms), release to match the BPM (at 125 BPM, 120 ms release = roughly 1/4 note), ratio 4:1 to 8:1
4. Set threshold so the compressor engages hard on each kick hit

Result: On every kick hit, the pad ducks immediately, then swells back up before the next kick. The pad sounds like it's breathing in time with the kick. This is the defining textural element of most commercial house music.

## Organ Stabs: The Classic House Sound

The organ stab is one of the oldest and most enduring sounds in house music — derived from gospel organ recordings, which early Chicago house producers sampled and repurposed.

In FL Studio, recreate organ stabs using:
- **FLEX**: Load a Hammond organ or gospel organ preset. Set the attack to near-zero (organs have a fast attack) and release to short (100–150 ms).
- **Sytrus**: Use an FM-based organ patch. The unique harmonic content of FM synthesis matches real organ character.
- **3xOsc**: Set all three oscillators to sine waves, detune slightly, use a fast attack and medium decay envelope.

Organ stabs in house are often processed with heavy chorus or rotary speaker simulation (the "Leslie" effect — the spinning speaker of the Hammond organ that creates a distinctive wobbling modulation). In FL Studio: add Fruity Chorus at high depth and moderate speed on the organ channel.
keyTakeaways: House stabs are short (1/16 length), rhythmically syncopated chords — their rhythm is as important as the harmony, and offbeat placement creates the groove tension against the four-on-the-floor kick.
Sidechain the pad to the kick so it pumps with the beat — fast attack, BPM-matched release (around 120 ms at 125 BPM), high ratio — this pumping is the defining texture of commercial house.
Combine short stabs (rhythmic foreground) with slow-attack sustained pads (harmonic background) for the layered house chord texture: punch from stabs, warmth from pads.
lessons.tryThis: In the Piano Roll at 125 BPM, programme a Cmaj7 stab pattern: notes at 1/16 length on the offbeat of beat 1 (step 3 in a 16-step bar), offbeat of beat 3 (step 11), and beat 4 (step 13). Run this over your four-on-the-floor kick pattern. Now add a pad on a separate channel playing the same Cmaj7 chord as a sustained whole note. Sidechain the pad compressor to the kick — set attack 2 ms, release 120 ms, ratio 6:1. Toggle the sidechain on and off and hear the pumping effect appear.
